God's design for Israel included not conquest alone, but testing. The nations left in Canaan were deliberate—a curriculum for faithfulness. The Lord did not remove every enemy to coddle His people, but left them so the next generation might learn warfare and, more importantly, obedience. This is the real test: Would Israel keep the commandments given through Moses, or would proximity to pagan nations erode their devotion? The answer comes quickly. They married the daughters of these nations, gave their own daughters to their sons, and served their gods. The line between separation and syncretism dissolved. They did not lose their identity through conquest; they lost it through compromise. Comfort and coexistence with idolatry proved more dangerous than any military threat. What began as living among the nations became serving their gods—and in doing so, Israel forgot the Lord their God.

This forgetting triggered the cycle that dominates Judges: evil, oppression, crying out, and deliverance. When Israel's anger kindled against them for abandoning His commandments, He sold them into the hand of Cushan-rishathaim. Sold—a word that strips away any illusion that Israel's slavery was accidental. God Himself handed them over as an act of justice and, paradoxically, mercy. The chains of oppression would teach what comfort could not: that abandoning the Lord results in bondage. Yet when they cried out, He did not abandon them. He raised up Othniel, upon whom the Spirit of the Lord came, and delivered Israel. Forty years of rest followed. But when Othniel died, the pattern repeated. They did evil again, and God gave Eglon king of Moab power over them. Eighteen years of subjugation—longer than the first oppression. The cycle deepens with each turn.

Ehud's assassination of Eglon is neither heroic glamour nor diplomatic subtlety. It is cunning, deception, and graphic violence. A left-handed judge hides a sword on his right thigh where a right-handed enemy would not expect it. He lies to the king, claims to bring a word from God, then murders him when they are alone. Ehud's act is effective—Israel seizes the fords of the Jordan, kills ten thousand Moabites, and the land rests for eighty years—but the text does not sentimentalize the method. God used Ehud's cunning just as He used Shamgar's oxgoad. Neither tool is refined, and neither the judge nor his method is idealized. What matters is that when Israel cried out, God raised up a deliverer and gave the victory. The land had rest. But the reader knows this rest will not last. Another generation will forget. Another cycle will begin.

This is the weight of Judges 3: the testing never stops. Faithfulness is not a single choice but a continuous one, renewed with each generation, complicated by proximity to those who worship other gods. Israel's problem is not their enemies outside but their hearts within. God leaves the nations in Canaan not to torment Israel but to expose what they truly love. Will they keep His commandments, or will the comfort of compromise prove more attractive than the call to separation? The answer, again and again, will be compromise. Yet even in this repeated failure, God does not abandon His people. He raises up judges, pours out His Spirit, and delivers them from the consequences of their choice—not because they deserve it, but because He remains faithful to His covenant, even when they do not.
